strategies.whatsHappening: The idea of what counts as a proper meal can add pressure that makes eating harder, especially on low-energy days.
tryThis: Build a short rotating list of meals that need minimal steps.
Keep those ingredients stocked as a standing priority.
Let go of any rule that a meal has to be cooked or elaborate to count.
Add variety slowly over time, without pressure to cook properly every day.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Expanding what qualifies as a real meal removes an unnecessary barrier between low energy and actually eating.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Support consistent eating and reduce mealtime decision fatigue.
strategies.source: Widely reported approach in disability and chronic illness communities.

strategies.title: Forgetting to Eat Until You're Shaking
strategies.situation: I've been working for six hours. My head hurts and I haven't had any water, and now I'm shaky and irritable.
strategies.internalThoughts: How do I keep letting this happen?
strategies.gentleReminder: Missing hunger cues during focused work isn't neglecting yourself on purpose. Focus can override the body's signals for hunger and thirst.
strategies.whatsHappening: During periods of concentration, internal signals like hunger can go unnoticed for hours. Relying on your body to interrupt you tends not to work reliably.
tryThis: Set a recurring alarm for meals and water, separate from other reminders.
Keep easy-to-eat snacks within reach of wherever you typically focus.
Use a marked water bottle so you can check your intake at a glance.
Treat the alarm as non-negotiable, even mid-task.
strategies.whyThisHelps: External reminders take over the job your internal hunger signals aren't reliably doing during focused states.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Reduce missed meals and prevent energy crashes from skipped eating.
strategies.source: Consistent with interoception research in autism and ADHD.

strategies.title: Food and Water on Autopilot
strategies.situation: I know I should drink more water and eat regularly, but I never remember until it's already a problem.
strategies.internalThoughts: This is such a basic thing. Everyone else just does it automatically.
strategies.gentleReminder: Needing external systems for something as basic as eating or drinking water isn't a sign of failure. For some brains these signals simply don't arrive automatically.
strategies.whatsHappening: Without a strong internal hunger or thirst signal, basic needs can slip through the cracks of a busy day entirely.
tryThis: Use a clear water bottle marked with time-based lines to track intake at a glance.
Set consistent meal times, rather than waiting to feel hungry.
Keep a visible snack supply somewhere you'll notice it throughout the day.
Pair eating or drinking with an existing habit.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Visual and scheduled cues externalize a signal your body isn't reliably providing.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Increase consistency of eating and hydration and reduce related energy crashes.
strategies.source: Widely reported strategy in ADHD and autism communities.

strategies.title: The Store Is Too Much
strategies.situation: Fifty types of cereal, buzzing lights, people everywhere. I feel like I'm going to burst into tears before I even reach the checkout.
strategies.internalThoughts: It's just grocery shopping. Why can't I handle something this ordinary?
strategies.gentleReminder: Finding an ordinary errand overwhelming isn't a sign something's wrong with you. Grocery stores pack in a huge amount of sensory input and decision-making at once.
strategies.whatsHappening: Shopping combines bright lighting, noise, crowds, and a high volume of small decisions all at the same time, which can add up fast even in a familiar store.
tryThis: Shop during quieter hours, such as early morning or right before closing.
Use a written list to reduce in-store decision-making.
Wear headphones or sunglasses if lighting and sound are the biggest triggers.
Use delivery or pickup options when the sensory cost feels too high.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Reducing sensory input and decision load, or avoiding the environment when possible, lowers the overall burden of a task that's more demanding than it appears.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Reduce shopping-related overwhelm and support more consistent grocery access.
strategies.source: Widely reported strategy in autism and ADHD communities.

strategies.title: Shopping On Your Terms
strategies.situation: I keep forcing myself to shop the normal way, even though I know it wears me out every single time.
strategies.internalThoughts: I feel like I should just be able to do this like everyone else.
strategies.gentleReminder: There's no single correct way to grocery shop, even though it can feel like there's an unspoken standard everyone else follows. Choosing what works for you is a reasonable adjustment.
strategies.whatsHappening: Conventional in-person shopping isn't the only valid way to keep a household stocked. Alternative approaches can meet the same need with far less strain.
tryThis: Consider a recurring delivery or pickup order for staple items.
Build a saved list you can reorder from with minimal new decisions each time.
Reserve in-person shopping for smaller, specific trips rather than the full weekly haul.
Let go of any idea that delivery is a lesser way to shop.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Shifting to a method with a lower sensory and decision-making cost makes the task sustainable long-term.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Reduce shopping-related exhaustion and increase consistency of household supplies.
strategies.source: Widely reported strategy in autism and ADHD communities.

strategies.title: Impulse Buys You Regret
strategies.situation: Buying this felt like the best idea in the world. Now I'm looking at my bank account and feeling sick.
strategies.internalThoughts: I'm so irresponsible with money.
strategies.gentleReminder: Impulse spending isn't a character flaw or proof of irresponsibility. A strong in-the-moment urge can genuinely override longer-term thinking, and that's a pattern, not a personal failing.
strategies.whatsHappening: In the moment a purchase can feel urgently rewarding, and that pull is often stronger than the more distant thought of a future bank balance.
tryThis: Add items to a cart or wishlist, but don't complete the purchase right away.
Set a fixed waiting period, such as 24 hours, before allowing yourself to buy.
Revisit the item after the wait and decide with a calmer perspective.
If you still want it and it fits your budget, that's a fine outcome too.
strategies.whyThisHelps: A built-in delay lets the initial dopamine-driven urge fade, giving your more considered judgment a real chance to weigh in.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Reduce regretted purchases and support more intentional spending.
strategies.source: Consistent with behavioral economics research on delay and impulse purchasing.

strategies.title: A System That Doesn't Rely on Willpower
strategies.situation: I keep trying to be better with money through sheer willpower, and it keeps not working.
strategies.internalThoughts: I just need more self-control.
strategies.gentleReminder: Willpower alone rarely holds up against a genuinely strong impulse, and that's true for most people. Needing structural support isn't a lesser way of managing money.
strategies.whatsHappening: Financial self-control that depends entirely on willpower has to hold up every time, with no room for a bad day. Building guardrails into the system removes some of that pressure.
tryThis: Set up automatic transfers to savings so the decision only has to be made once.
Use separate accounts for spending versus essential bills.
Set spending alerts on your accounts or cards.
Remove saved payment details from apps that make impulse buying frictionless.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Structural guardrails reduce how often willpower has to be the only thing standing between you and an impulsive decision.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Increase financial stability and reduce reliance on willpower alone.
strategies.source: Consistent with behavioral economics research on choice architecture and friction.

strategies.title: Money Conversations You Keep Avoiding
strategies.situation: I know I need to talk to my partner about money, but I keep putting it off. Just thinking about the conversation makes my stomach drop.
strategies.internalThoughts: What if this turns into a fight, or they think less of me?
strategies.gentleReminder: Avoiding a money conversation isn't financial irresponsibility. Money talks carry a lot of emotional weight, and dreading a conversation that combines vulnerability and numbers is reasonable.
strategies.whatsHappening: Financial conversations often mix practical details with sensitive emotional territory. Avoiding the conversation doesn't remove the issue, it just delays it.
tryThis: Pick a specific, low-pressure time to raise it, rather than waiting for a perfect moment.
Prepare a simple opening line in advance.
Focus the first conversation on one specific topic, rather than trying to cover everything.
If speaking feels too hard, consider starting the conversation in writing.
strategies.whyThisHelps: A prepared, narrow opening reduces the size of the conversation you're bracing for, making it easier to actually start.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Reduce avoidance-related financial stress and support clearer financial communication.
strategies.source: Consistent with financial therapy and couples communication approaches.

strategies.title: Missed Calls, Missed Appointments
strategies.situation: I had an appointment today and completely missed it. I didn't even remember until it was already over.
strategies.internalThoughts: How do I keep letting this happen? I feel like such a mess.
strategies.gentleReminder: Missing appointments repeatedly, despite genuinely intending to go, isn't carelessness. Memory alone often isn't a reliable enough system for something time-sensitive.
strategies.whatsHappening: Appointments rely on remembering a specific time on a specific day, often set weeks in advance, which is exactly the kind of detail memory can lose track of.
tryThis: Enter every appointment into a calendar the moment it's scheduled.
Set at least two reminders, one the day before, one an hour before.
Keep appointments in one central calendar rather than scattered across multiple places.
If a reminder system has failed before, add redundancy rather than blaming yourself.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Layered reminders reduce the dependence on any single moment of memory working correctly.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Reduce missed appointments and reduce related stress and rescheduling.
strategies.source: Consistent with executive function coaching approaches to external scheduling systems.

strategies.title: Building a Reminder Net
strategies.situation: I have reminders set, but I still miss things because they don't feel urgent enough when they pop up.
strategies.internalThoughts: I have a system, so why doesn't it actually work?
strategies.gentleReminder: A single reminder not being enough doesn't mean your system is broken beyond repair. One alert can easily get dismissed or lost in the moment.
strategies.whatsHappening: A single reminder assumes you'll be in the right state of mind to fully register and act on it. Layering multiple reminders builds in redundancy.
tryThis: Set reminders at multiple intervals, a few days before, the day before, and an hour before.
Use more than one format, like a calendar alert plus a phone reminder.
Where possible, ask a trusted person to be a backup reminder for high-stakes appointments.
Review your calendar at a consistent time each day as an additional layer.
strategies.whyThisHelps: A layered system means missing one reminder doesn't mean missing the whole appointment.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Reduce missed appointments and increase confidence in your scheduling system.
strategies.source: Consistent with executive function coaching approaches to redundant reminder systems.

strategies.title: Starting a Task No One's Watching
strategies.situation: This chore has been sitting on my list for weeks. No one's checking on it, so it just keeps getting pushed back.
strategies.internalThoughts: If it actually mattered, I'd have done it by now.
strategies.gentleReminder: A task being easy to indefinitely postpone doesn't mean it doesn't matter. Some tasks simply lack the external pressure that makes starting easier.
strategies.whatsHappening: Tasks with a deadline or an audience tend to get done because there's an external trigger pushing you toward action. Tasks with no witness lack that trigger entirely.
tryThis: Create an artificial deadline for the task, even if no one else will notice it.
Tell someone else you're planning to do it, to add a small amount of accountability.
Schedule it into your calendar like an appointment.
Pair it with something you're already doing that day.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Manufacturing the external pressure that's naturally missing gives the task the kind of trigger it needs to actually get started.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Increase completion of low-urgency household tasks.
strategies.source: Widely reported strategy in ADHD coaching and productivity communities.

strategies.title: The First Five Minutes of a Chore
strategies.situation: I know exactly what chore needs doing. I just can't seem to make myself start it.
strategies.internalThoughts: It's not even that hard. Why can't I just do it?
strategies.gentleReminder: A task not being objectively hard doesn't mean starting it will feel easy. Difficulty starting and difficulty of the task itself are two separate things.
strategies.whatsHappening: The hardest part of most chores is the transition from not-doing to doing, not the chore itself once it's underway.
tryThis: Commit only to the first five minutes, with explicit permission to stop after.
Set a timer so the commitment has a clear, visible end point.
Start with the smallest, most concrete first action.
Notice that continuing often feels easier once you're already moving.
strategies.whyThisHelps: A small, time-limited commitment lowers the barrier enough to get past the hardest part, starting, without requiring commitment to the whole task upfront.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Increase task initiation for household chores and reduce chronic avoidance.
strategies.source: Widely reported approach in ADHD coaching communities.

strategies.title: Awake Long After You Meant to Sleep
strategies.situation: I stayed up until 4am again, even though I meant to sleep hours earlier. Half the day is gone and I feel like a failure.
strategies.internalThoughts: I should have more self-discipline about my bedtime.
strategies.gentleReminder: A delayed sleep schedule isn't a discipline problem. For a lot of neurodivergent people, the body's internal clock genuinely runs later than a standard schedule expects.
strategies.whatsHappening: Sleep timing is regulated partly by light exposure and internal rhythm, not purely by intention. Adjusting the cues your body uses tends to be more effective than willpower.
tryThis: Wake up at a consistent time, even after a late night, rather than trying to catch up by sleeping in.
Get direct sunlight or bright light within the first 30 minutes of waking.
Dim lights and reduce screens in the hour before your target bedtime.
Make changes gradually, shifting bedtime by 15 minutes at a time.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Using light to guide your internal clock works with your body's actual rhythm-setting mechanism, rather than relying on willpower to override it.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Gradually shift sleep schedule earlier and improve sleep consistency.
strategies.source: Consistent with sleep science research on light exposure and circadian rhythm regulation.

strategies.title: Winding Down Without a Timer
strategies.situation: I keep trying to just go to bed earlier, but I never actually feel sleepy at the time I've decided I should.
strategies.internalThoughts: If I could just make myself tired on cue, this would be so much easier.
strategies.gentleReminder: Not feeling sleepy on command isn't something you can will into happening. Sleepiness responds to cues and routines building up over time, not to a decision made in the moment.
strategies.whatsHappening: The body tends to rely on a consistent pattern of cues to signal sleep is coming, rather than responding instantly to a chosen bedtime.
tryThis: Build a short, repeatable wind-down sequence, the same two or three steps each night.
Keep the sequence low-stimulation, dim lighting and quiet activity.
Start the sequence at a consistent time, rather than waiting until you already feel tired.
Give the routine time to work, consistency matters more than any single night.
strategies.whyThisHelps: A repeated sequence of cues teaches your body to associate that pattern with approaching sleep, rather than relying on willpower in the moment.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Improve ease of falling asleep and support more consistent sleep timing.
strategies.source: Consistent with sleep hygiene research on pre-sleep routines and conditioned cues.

strategies.title: Running on Empty for Months
strategies.situation: I've felt exhausted for months now, and rest doesn't seem to fix it anymore. Even things I used to enjoy feel like too much.
strategies.internalThoughts: I should be able to push through this like I always have. What's wrong with me?
strategies.gentleReminder: Exhaustion that doesn't respond to a good night's sleep or a weekend off isn't a sign you're not trying hard enough. It's often a sign this has moved past ordinary tiredness.
strategies.whatsHappening: Neurodivergent burnout is deep depletion that builds up over a long period, often from sustained effort to meet demands your brain wasn't designed to meet without support.
tryThis: Notice if rest that used to help isn't helping anymore, that's useful information.
Reduce demands wherever genuinely possible, even temporarily.
Focus on the absolute basics first, sleep, food, water, before anything else.
Consider talking to a doctor or therapist familiar with neurodivergent burnout.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Recognizing burnout as a distinct state, rather than ordinary tiredness, changes what kind of response actually helps.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Begin genuine recovery and reduce the cycle of pushing through and crashing further.
strategies.source: Neurodivergent burnout is documented in emerging autism and ADHD research literature.

strategies.title: This Isn't Laziness, It's Burnout
strategies.situation: I can't get myself to do things I used to manage easily. People keep telling me to just push through, and I feel like I'm failing.
strategies.internalThoughts: Maybe I really am just lazy.
strategies.gentleReminder: A sudden drop in your ability to do things you used to manage isn't laziness, it's one of the clearest signs of burnout, and it deserves to be treated as a real, depleted state.
strategies.whatsHappening: Burnout can genuinely reduce your capacity for things that used to be manageable, not because motivation has disappeared, but because the underlying resources have run out.
tryThis: Notice the specific things that feel harder than they used to, without judgment.
Say the reframe out loud if it helps, that this isn't laziness, your capacity is lower right now.
Lower your expectations of yourself temporarily, rather than matching your previous baseline.
Share the reframe with people close to you if it feels safe to do so.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Correctly naming burnout, instead of laziness, points you toward rest and reduced demand rather than more pushing.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Reduce shame around reduced capacity and support appropriate recovery response.
strategies.source: Consistent with emerging research distinguishing burnout-related capacity loss from motivational deficits.

strategies.title: Exhausted From Being "On" All Day
strategies.situation: By the end of the day, I have nothing left, even though nothing dramatic happened. I just spent all day appearing normal.
strategies.internalThoughts: I didn't even do anything that hard. Why am I this drained?
strategies.gentleReminder: Feeling drained after a day that didn't look objectively hard isn't disproportionate. Appearing on or neurotypical for hours takes genuine, sustained effort.
strategies.whatsHappening: Masking requires continuously monitoring and adjusting how you speak, move, and react, often without conscious awareness. That ongoing self-monitoring uses real energy.
tryThis: Build in unmasked time as a regular, expected part of your day.
Notice which environments require the most performance, and reduce time in them where possible.
After a high-masking day, prioritize low-demand recovery over trying to be productive.
Let go of the expectation that a day without dramatic events should also be an easy day.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Recognizing masking as real, ongoing work explains the exhaustion and points toward the kind of rest that actually addresses it.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Reduce cumulative masking exhaustion and support more accurate energy expectations.
strategies.source: Masking-related exhaustion is documented in autism and ADHD research literature.

strategies.title: The Cost of Performing Normal
strategies.situation: I've never really thought about what masking actually costs me. I just assumed everyone gets tired at the end of the day.
strategies.internalThoughts: Maybe this is just what being tired feels like for everyone.
strategies.gentleReminder: Not realizing how much masking is costing you isn't a lack of insight. Masking can become so automatic that its toll blends into the background of daily life.
strategies.whatsHappening: Masking's cost often goes unrecognized because it's compared to a baseline that doesn't reflect how much effort is actually involved.
tryThis: List a few specific things you do to mask, like suppressing a stim or forcing certain expressions.
For each one, notice roughly how much effort it takes, even if it's automatic.
Compare a masked day to a day where you had more room to be yourself.
Use this awareness to plan more deliberate recovery time after high-masking days.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Making the specific costs of masking visible helps you plan realistic recovery instead of comparing your exhaustion to an inaccurate baseline.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Increase awareness of masking's true cost and support more accurate energy planning.
strategies.source: Consistent with autism and ADHD research on the cognitive and emotional cost of masking.

strategies.title: Recovery Isn't Wasted Time
strategies.situation: I spent the whole afternoon doing nothing productive, and now I feel guilty about it instead of rested.
strategies.internalThoughts: That time could have been used for something useful.
strategies.gentleReminder: Feeling guilty after resting doesn't mean the rest wasn't needed. Recovery is doing something, even when it doesn't produce a visible result.
strategies.whatsHappening: Recovery time restores the capacity that gets used up by daily demands, including ones that aren't obvious from the outside, like masking or sensory strain.
tryThis: Reframe recovery time as an investment in future capacity, not a gap in your day.
Notice how you feel and function differently after genuine rest versus pushing through.
Give yourself permission to rest before you're at the point of complete depletion.
If guilt shows up, name it directly, that the guilt doesn't mean the rest was wrong.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Reframing rest as functional, rather than something to justify, reduces the guilt that keeps people pushing past their limits.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Reduce recovery-related guilt and support more consistent, effective rest.
strategies.source: Consistent with occupational therapy and burnout literature on the functional role of rest.

strategies.title: What Actual Rest Looks Like
strategies.situation: I spent hours scrolling on my phone, and I still feel just as drained as before. I don't understand why resting isn't working.
strategies.internalThoughts: I gave myself a break, so why don't I feel any better?
strategies.gentleReminder: Not feeling restored after what looked like downtime doesn't mean something's wrong with your ability to rest. Some activities that look like rest don't actually restore the depleted capacity.
strategies.whatsHappening: Passive activities like scrolling can occupy time without genuinely reducing the sensory or cognitive load driving the exhaustion. Real recovery often needs lower input.
tryThis: Notice how you feel after different kinds of downtime, like scrolling versus quiet time versus time outdoors.
Experiment with genuinely low-input rest, like sitting quietly or lying down without a screen.
Give a new form of rest a fair trial before deciding it doesn't work for you.
Let go of any assumption that rest has to involve entertainment to count.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Identifying which activities genuinely lower your sensory and cognitive load, rather than just occupying time, helps you choose rest that actually restores capacity.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Increase effectiveness of rest and reduce time spent on downtime that doesn't actually recover energy.
strategies.source: Consistent with occupational therapy approaches distinguishing passive activity from genuine recovery.

strategies.title: Spending Energy You Don't Have
strategies.situation: I keep committing to things as if I have unlimited energy, and then I'm completely wiped out by things I didn't even plan for.
strategies.internalThoughts: I should know my limits better by now.
strategies.gentleReminder: Running on a deficit without noticing isn't a failure to know yourself. Energy levels for a lot of neurodivergent people can shift quickly and unpredictably.
strategies.whatsHappening: Without a clear sense of how much capacity you're working with, it's easy to commit as though today's energy will match yesterday's.
tryThis: Each morning, take thirty seconds to rate your energy on a simple scale.
Use that rating to inform what you commit to for the day.
Notice patterns over time, certain days or triggers that consistently lower your energy.
Build in a small buffer of unplanned time, rather than scheduling every available hour.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Treating energy as something to check and track, rather than assume, gives you real information to plan around instead of guessing.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Reduce overcommitment and increase alignment between plans and actual capacity.
strategies.source: Consistent with occupational therapy energy conservation and pacing approaches.

strategies.title: A Budget for Your Battery
strategies.situation: I plan my day around time, but time doesn't actually predict how I'll feel or what I'll be able to do.
strategies.internalThoughts: I have the hours available, so why can't I get through my list?
strategies.gentleReminder: Having enough hours in the day doesn't guarantee having enough energy to fill them. Time and energy are genuinely different resources.
strategies.whatsHappening: A schedule built purely around clock time assumes a flat, consistent level of energy throughout the day, which doesn't reflect how energy actually fluctuates.
tryThis: Label tasks by the energy level they require instead of scheduling by hour.
Match higher-energy tasks to the times of day you tend to have more capacity.
Save low-energy tasks for times you know tend to be more depleted.
Adjust the plan in real time based on your actual energy that day.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Planning around energy, instead of time alone, accounts for the resource that actually determines what you can realistically get done.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Increase realistic task completion and reduce end-of-day exhaustion from misaligned planning.
strategies.source: Consistent with occupational therapy energy conservation techniques.

strategies.title: All-or-Nothing Days
strategies.situation: On good days I do everything at once, and then I crash for days afterward. I can't seem to find a middle ground.
strategies.internalThoughts: I should just take it easy on the good days too, but it feels impossible not to make the most of them.
strategies.gentleReminder: The pull to do everything on a good day isn't overexcitement or poor judgment. A burst of energy can feel rare and urgent, making it genuinely hard to hold back.
strategies.whatsHappening: Alternating between doing too much and too little often comes from treating high-energy days as an exception to make the most of, rather than part of a longer pattern.
tryThis: On a good-energy day, set a deliberate cap on how much you'll take on.
Save some of that energy for the following day, rather than spending all of it at once.
Notice the urge to make the most of it, and treat it as a signal to pace, not push further.
Track how a paced good day affects the days that follow, compared to an all-out one.
strategies.whyThisHelps: Deliberately capping output on high-energy days protects against the crash that often follows, supporting a steadier pattern over time.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Reduce boom-and-bust energy cycles and increase overall consistency.
strategies.source: Consistent with occupational therapy pacing approaches used in chronic illness and energy-limiting conditions.

strategies.title: Small and Steady Beats All-Out
strategies.situation: I always go all-in on things, and I always end up burning out. I don't know how to do things differently.
strategies.internalThoughts: If I don't go all-in, will I even get anything done?
strategies.gentleReminder: Worrying that pacing yourself means getting less done is reasonable, but it's worth questioning, since an all-out approach that ends in burnout often produces less over time.
strategies.whatsHappening: Going all-in can feel productive because the short-term output is high, but the resulting crash often erases those gains and then some.
tryThis: Pick one recurring task or goal and deliberately choose a smaller, steadier version of it.
Track your output over a few weeks, comparing steady pacing to your usual all-or-nothing approach.
Resist the urge to escalate just because a steady pace feels underwhelming in the moment.
Remind yourself that avoiding a crash is itself a form of progress.
strategies.whyThisHelps: A steady, sustainable pace avoids the crash that typically erases the gains made during an all-out burst, producing more reliable results over time.
strategies.expectedOutcome: Increase long-term consistency and reduce burnout cycles.
strategies.source: Consistent with occupational therapy pacing approaches used in chronic illness and energy-limiting conditions.
